title: Creating Threads
ques: How do you create and start a new **Thread** that runs a method `PrintNumbers`?
explanation: `System.Threading.Thread` represents an OS thread. `Start()` begins execution concurrently.

title: Sleep
ques: How do you pause the current thread for 1 second (1000 milliseconds)?
explanation: `Thread.Sleep` suspends the current thread for the specified amount of time.

title: Join
ques: What does `thread.Join()` do? Write a snippet where the main thread waits for `t` to finish.
explanation: `Join` blocks the calling thread until the thread represented by the instance terminates.

title: Lock Keyword
ques: Use the **`lock`** keyword to protect a shared resource `count` from race conditions.
explanation: `lock(obj)` ensures that only one thread can execute the block of code at a time. It acquires a mutual exclusion lock on the object.

title: Task Parallel Library (TPL)
ques: Start a **Task** that runs a lambda expression printing "Task Run". Use `Task.Run`.
explanation: Tasks are higher-level abstractions than Threads. `Task.Run` queues work to run on the ThreadPool.

title: Async and Await
ques: Define an `async` method `GetDataAsync` that waits for 2 seconds (simulating I/O) using `await Task.Delay`.
explanation: `async` marks a method as asynchronous. `await` pauses the execution of the method until the awaited task completes, without blocking the thread.

title: Returning Values from Async
ques: How do you return an `int` from an async method? Method signature for returning 42.
explanation: Use `Task<TResult>` as the return type. The result is wrapped in the task.

title: Waiting for Multiple Tasks
ques: Use **`Task.WhenAll`** to wait for two tasks `t1` and `t2` to complete.
explanation: `Task.WhenAll` creates a task that completes when all the supplied tasks have completed.

title: BackgroundWorker (Legacy)
ques: What is `BackgroundWorker` primarily used for in WinForms (historical context)?

answer.content: It allows running operations on a separate thread and safely reporting progress or completion back to the **UI thread** (via `ReportProgress` and `RunWorkerCompleted`).

explanation: Modern UI apps prefer `async/await`, but `BackgroundWorker` was the standard for responsive UIs in older .NET versions.

title: Thread vs Task
ques: Briefly explain why **Tasks** are generally preferred over manual **Threads**.

answer.content: Tasks use the system **ThreadPool**, which is more efficient (reuses threads) than creating new OS threads. Tasks also provide better API for chaining, waiting, and return values (`async/await`).

explanation: Creating a Thread is expensive (stack memory). Tasks optimize resource usage.
